Consider whether simple diagrams or existing imagery might serve your purpose before generating new visuals Be strategic with built-in AI features: Many common tools (e.g., search engines, word processors) now include AI by default. Check whether you can turn off or limit these features in the settings menu. When you can’t disable them entirely, choose the lowest-impact option. For example, use standard search instead of AI-generated overviews, or traditional spellcheck instead of AI rewriting Match the tool to the task: General-purpose LLMs are the most common starting point for new AI users, but they are more resource-intensive than smaller, task-specific Technologies. Once you have a clearer sense of your specific use cases, consider adopting task-specific or domain-specific tools that run on smaller models. These lighter AI Technologies are often more efficient and can perform targeted tasks such as grammar checking, formatting citations, or data analysis with a much lower environmental impact. Consider which settings you enable for which tasks and avoid defaulting to the most advanced options unless they are necessary. For example, extended reasoning settings can improve accuracy when synthesizing findings across multiple papers, but for straightforward text editing or summarizing a short abstract, leaving this off will save energy without reducing quality Understand task complexity levels: Just as summarizing many papers takes you more effort than summarizing one, the same can be said for AI. The more complex the task, the more computing resources and energy it requires Different research tasks benefit from different levels of AI assistance. Understanding these categories helps you choose the most effective approach: Level of intensity Research task example Light Proofreading text, grammar and spell checking, formatting references, generating titles or keywords Moderate Running AI-assisted search, drafting short sections of text, generating code, summarizing short documents, converting tables between formats (for example: CSV to JSON to LaTeX) Heavy Synthesizing findings across many papers, conducting literature reviews that pull papers from external databases, analyzing long documents or large datasets, creating research figures or images Very heavy Running autonomous workflows that complete multi-step tasks without supervision, producing video abstracts or animations, creating 3D models or simulations Navigating AI policies across institutions, funders, publishers, and journals Research institutions, funding agencies, publishers, and journals may each impose restrictions on how AI Technologies can be used in research and publication. Institutions: Many universities and research organizations issue AI policies that govern how their researchers may use these Technologies. If you’re uncertain, consult your research office for clarification Funders: Grant agreements may include specific requirements or restrictions on AI use in conducting or disseminating research. Always review your funding terms to ensure compliance Publishers and journals: Editorial policies vary in what types of AI use they permit. If your use of AI does not align with a journal’s policy, you may be asked to revise your manuscript to remove or rewrite AI-assisted sections. While this may feel inconvenient, disclosure and documentation brings transparency to the scholarly record How can I navigate conflicting AI guidance? The landscape of AI guidance for authors is rapidly evolving as institutions, funders, and journals work to develop policies that balance innovation with upholding publication standards and research integrity. This can create uncertainty about how AI may be used in your work and what must be declared. Rather than trying to anticipate every possible requirement, focus on transparency and documentation. Maintaining clear records of your AI use allows you to adapt disclosures to meet different journal, funder, or institutional requirements without compromising integrity. We recommend that you: Review your institution’s AI policy and note the date when you last checked it Check the specific requirements of your target journal, as policies vary across publishers and titles Document your AI use consistently throughout your project so you can adapt disclosures as needed (AI Technology and version, month accessed, why it was used) When in doubt, follow the most restrictive policy, as this is generally the safest approach Always comply with the terms of your author agreement concerning AI and intellectual property and other matters Over time, we expect policies to become clearer and more aligned as the research community gains experience with AI. In the meantime, this document is designed to help you navigate the current uncertainty with practical and actionable strategies. Is it safe to put my unpublished content into AI Technologies? The question of content security and AI Technologies is complex and continuously evolving. You should carefully consider both the privacy implications and legal aspects of sharing content, as Technologies may have different terms regarding content rights and usage. Take extra caution when sharing potentially sensitive materials that may be retained by AI models. Sensitive materials include: Data protected by regulations (like HIPAA or FERPA) Private institutional or personal information Content subject to confidentiality agreements or embargoes Novel research findings or unique methodologies When working with sensitive content, remove private details from text or data sets before using publicly available generative AI Technologies. Most publicly available Technologies have limited privacy controls, while professional or paid versions may include privacy settings to opt out of data training or to request data deletion. If your institution provides access to enterprise versions of AI, these offer the most robust privacy controls and are the safest option for handling sensitive data. Regardless of the AI Technology version, always prioritize those that explicitly state they do not retain or train on user data. AI in peer review Can I use AI to help me review manuscripts? Authors’ trust in peer review depends on maintaining strict confidentiality of unpublished work. Manuscripts under review are confidential and must not be uploaded to AI Technologies, whether in full or in part. Authors have not given permission for reviewers or editors to share their unpublished work with AI Technologies. However, there are meaningful ways reviewers can use AI while protecting manuscript confidentiality. What editors and reviewers can do safely: Use AI to organize your thoughts and prioritize feedback Use AI to improve review clarity and tone or translate your review Ask AI general questions about research methods or statistical approaches without referencing manuscript specifics Wiley is actively developing new approaches that will expand these possibilities while maintaining the confidentiality expected in review. See the What’s next for AI in peer review author FAQ for more information. Do I need to disclose AI use to the author or editor? We require disclosure of AI when it’s used for drafting assistance in your reviews. When disclosing AI use in your review, we recommend sharing: AI Technology's name and version Date/year of use AI Technology's role in drafting or editing assistance The author’s role in directing or reviewing the work of the AI Technology Example: "GPT-5 was used to prioritize and polish review feedback. All original review feedback was provided by the peer reviewer, and all AI-generated content was reviewed, revised, and approved by the peer reviewer prior to sending this review.” How should editors and reviewers respond to AI use in manuscripts? At Wiley, AI use in manuscript development is not grounds for rejection. Editorial evaluation should focus on the quality, integrity, disclosure, and clarity of communication in the research, regardless of the Technologies used to support manuscript development. We encourage authors to use AI responsibly when it helps them communicate their research more effectively and advance scientific discovery. Rather than asking “was AI used here?”, the more important questions are: “Does this manuscript meet the editorial policies of my journal and the expectations of its readers? and “Are the disclosures regarding AI use consistent with what’s presented in the manuscript?” By responding to AI use with clarity and fairness, editors help create a culture that encourages transparency, supports innovation, and strengthens trust in the peer review process. If concerns arise during review, editors should: Start with author communication: If you notice issues that concern you, or concerns are raised to you by a peer reviewer—whether you suspect AI involvement or not—reach out to the author to discuss them directly. Focus your conversation on the specific problems you've identified and seek clarity from the author Seek support: Wiley’s Integrity Assurance and Case Resolution team can assist your evaluation ( researchintegrity@wiley.com ). Common issues that warrant editorial review: Citations that don't adequately support claims Unclear methodology or analysis Poor logical flow or organization Missing context or background information Writing quality that doesn't meet your journal's standards Evidence of integrity concerns that may warrant rejection: Fabricated citations or references Falsified data or methodology Inability to verify research when requested Failure to address substantive concerns about research validity In the event of potential integrity concerns, editors should follow their journal’s and Wiley’s standard policies and processes for handling them and consult with Wiley’s Integrity Assurance and Case Resolution team as needed. By handling concerns in this way, editors ensure manuscripts are evaluated fairly while helping the community adapt confidently to new Technologies. What's next for AI in peer review? The peer review system continues to evolve in response to ongoing needs: finding qualified reviewers, managing increasing submission volumes, maintaining quality standards, and addressing increasingly sophisticated integrity challenges. AI Technologies are already helping address some of these areas and will likely play a larger role in the future. Existing AI developments Wiley’s Research Exchange platform uses AI to support editors with reviewer matching and integrity screening. Reviewer matching: AI Technologies are used to match manuscripts with appropriate reviewers based on expertise, availability, and review history. These Technologies provide editors with a more efficient way to identify potential reviewers Integrity screening: AI Technologies are being used to detect plagiarism, image manipulation, and other research integrity issues during the editorial process. These Technologies flag specific markers for integrity issues so that editors can review the article with this information in mind Potential AI applications for the future Wiley is committed to continuous improvement in effective peer review, which includes the exploration of AI Technologies to support challenges that editors, reviewers, and authors face during review. Confidential review assistance: AI Technologies that can assist editors and reviewers with manuscript analysis within secure editorial environments, ensuring that no confidential information is shared with external parties AI-guided review processes: Systems that could prompt reviewers with field-specific questions or help ensure comprehensive coverage of key evaluation criteria Quality enhancement: AI Technologies that help reviewers structure feedback more effectively, improve tone and politeness, or identify when reviews lack substantive engagement with the research These developments will enhance peer review effectiveness while preserving the expert judgment and scholarly engagement that make peer review valuable. Getting started with AI Technologies How can I test and compare AI Technologies? Testing AI Technologies before adopting them is essential for determining whether your chosen AI Technology supports your research needs without compromising your standards. Because all AI Technologies behave and perform differently, we recommend trying multiple options using the checklist below. The goal is not to find a "perfect" Technology, but to identify Technologies that provide trustworthy support for your research without compromising your intellectual control or research standards. To learn more about the types of Technologies and capabilities currently available, see What types of AI Technologies are available for research authors? and What capabilities should I look for in AI Technologies? Be aware that how you phrase your request to the AI (also called prompting) has a direct impact on the results you receive. Practice different ways to ask the AI to perform tasks and review how it responds. Look for tutorials on prompting strategies to understand how others are successfully using prompting techniques to interact with AI. When you begin testing AI Technologies, start with simple, low-risk tasks and increase complexity as your familiarity grows. Basic testing recommendations Start with familiar tasks: Begin by assigning the AI a task you can complete yourself. This helps you judge whether the AI Technology’s performance is acceptable compared to your own standards Compare multiple tools: Different Technologies may excel with different tasks. Comparing AI Technologies by running them through similar prompting exercises will help you understand their strengths, weaknesses, and unique features Keep track of your results (optional): Some researchers find it useful to keep notes on what they tested, how Technologies performed, and which prompts or settings produced strong results. This can be especially valuable if you’re comparing several tools or need to document your methodology. However, many researchers quickly develop preferences through regular use without maintaining a detailed log AI testing - starter checklist 1. Test accuracy in your field Ask the AI to explain 2-3 concepts from your research area. ☐ Are concepts and definitions accurate and appropriate for your field? ☐ Are terms used correctly? 2. Test source reliability Ask the AI to find papers and verify responses against primary sources. ☐ Are the links provided accurate and relevant? ☐ If citations are provided, are the authors and references real and accurate? ☐ Do citations support the claims as the AI suggests? ☐ Does the AI output over-represent certain voices or sources of information? ☐ Can the tool explain how it arrived at its results or which sources it used? 3. Test privacy, security, and data settings Check the account settings and terms of service for data use policies. ☐ Can you control data retention and training? ☐ Are the terms acceptable for your research needs? 4. Test file format compatibility Upload non-sensitive documents—such as published papers, public datasets, or sample text from your field. Note: Be aware that AI Technology may train on or learn from any content you share. ☐ Does the AI support your most commonly used file types? ☐ When asked about the file, does the AI provide accurate responses? 5. Test output capabilities Ask the AI to provide example outputs for you to assess, for example, “create a table comparing X and Y” or “format this citation in APA style”. ☐ How closely does the output match your request? ☐ Is the AI responsive to your feedback (critique, clarification, encouragement)? ☐ How many rounds of prompting are required to get the expected output? 6. Test practical limitations Work with the AI for an extended period to understand session constraints. ☐ Does the AI session last long enough for your typical research tasks? ☐ Does it retain context throughout the conversation? ☐ Can you manage conversation history (save, delete, restart)? 7. Test collaboration and workflow integration Try saving and sharing outputs and test if the tool integrates with your commonly used software platforms. ☐ Can you share outputs with colleagues in a usable format? ☐ Can you export or share AI conversation logs? ☐ Can the AI tool be used alongside your lab notebooks, reference managers, or other research tools? 8. Assess cost-benefit after testing Start testing by using free versions of AI Technology to understand capabilities. For the AI Technology you prefer, research what additional features are available in paid versions, which often provide better privacy controls and enhanced capabilities. ☐ Do the benefits justify the cost in your context? Choosing the right AI Technologies for your tasks The "best" AI Technology depends on your specific research needs, institutional context, and personal workflow preferences. This framework helps you make systematic, informed decisions rather than following general recommendations. After testing, consider: Which Technologies you prefer for exploratory tasks such as generating ideas or challenging assumptions Which Technologies you rely on for more structured work such as data analysis, manuscript formatting, or literature summaries Which Technologies consistently fall short and can be set aside The goal is to build a set of tools that support your work without replacing your expertise and to understand where each one adds value to your work. What types of AI Technologies are available for research authors? There are many types of AI Technologies available to support research and writing tasks. This section will help you be aware of the options available so you can explore what works best for different tasks. You don’t need to have an in-depth understanding of these options to get started. Most authors start with a basic LLM and gradually explore more specialized options as their needs become clearer. General purpose Large Language Models LLMs are versatile, conversational AI Technologies trained on vast datasets. They enable users to ask questions, generate ideas, and refine content through interactive dialogue. Most LLMs aren’t trained specifically for research tasks or with research data, so their output may be incomplete or out of date. Because they can generate convincing but inaccurate information, always verify factual claims and citations against trusted sources. Common use cases include: Exploring potential research questions or frameworks Summarizing long documents or simplifying complex ideas Drafting emails, abstracts, or standard sections of manuscripts Task-specific AI Technologies These AI Technologies are designed for defined functions such as citation management, plagiarism detection, transcription and note taking, or grammar checking. Because they are built for narrow purposes, they often outperform general LLMs in accuracy and usability. Many also offer guided workflows and integration with existing research software, making them accessible for authors without AI expertise. Domain-specific scientific AI Technologies These AI Technologies are trained on scientific data or designed with embedded subject knowledge, making them more suitable for technical research applications. They may support experimental design, data analysis, lab management, molecular modeling, protein structure prediction, or visualization of complex systems. These AI Technologies require domain knowledge to use effectively. This category is rapidly evolving, and availability varies significantly by research domain. In some cases, researchers are also developing custom AI Technologies where no commercial solution exists. AI-integrated features in established research tools Many research tools now include built-in AI features that help with writing, visualization, or project management. Because they are embedded within trusted platforms, these features often offer stronger privacy controls and institutional compliance. They can also offer a low-friction way to experiment with AI without adopting an entirely new software platform. What capabilities should I look for in AI Technologies? As AI Technologies evolve, so do their capabilities. Advanced features such as extended reasoning, real-time tool access, and independent decision-making are enabling researchers and authors to use AI in new ways. Practical capabilities like source transparency and workflow compatibility address common pain points and make AI easier to use effectively. These capabilities can appear across any of the AI technologies described above—from basic LLMs to specialized technologies for research purposes. Extended reasoning AI Technologies with extended reasoning are designed to help you think through complex problems in structured ways. They can break down multifaceted research questions in systematic analytical steps, suggest methodological frameworks or research design elements, and help organize complex arguments. They can effectively process and compare multiple research papers, datasets, or documents simultaneously to support thematic analysis or identify gaps. You may have heard these referred to as: reasoning models, advanced reasoning, deep reasoning, or chain-of-thought. AI Technologies with this capability act more like thinking partners than assistants. They excel at systematic analysis, while you provide domain expertise, contextual judgment, and final decision-making authority. Look for Technologies that can: Walk through step-by-step reasoning processes Analyze gaps in logic or research design Offer structured critiques or counterarguments Help organize or scaffold complex arguments Compare sources to support synthesis Extended reasoning expands your analytical range while keeping intellectual leadership and accountability with you. Real-time access to external tools and data Some AI platforms can interact with live sources such as search engines, APIs, code environments, or databases. Rather than relying solely on pre-trained knowledge, these AI Technologies retrieve current and source-verifiable data in real time. You may have heard these referred to as: web-enabled AI, RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation), tool calling, or live data access. Look for Technologies that can: Search the internet or scholarly databases during your session Retrieve and combine current data for analysis Cross-reference claims across sources and provide live links Execute code or run queries to produce verifiable results Real-time access supports more reliable verification, broader exploration, and relevance in fast-moving fields. Independent decision-making AI Technologies with independent decision-making capabilities can work through complex research tasks without requiring you to direct each step. Instead of prompting at every stage, you can set a research goal and allow the AI to work systematically toward it while you focus on other tasks. You may have heard these referred to as: agentic AI, autonomous agents, or agent workflows. They are most valuable for multi-source or multi-step projects that would otherwise require hours of coordination. Strong tools in this category should provide progress updates and transparent reporting so you can monitor, verify, and guide their work. Look for tools that can: Conduct systematic searches across multiple databases and compile structured source lists Organize large amounts of research material into usable overviews Develop experimental protocols by analyzing existing methods and adapting them to your needs Coordinate multi-step projects, such as sequential data collection and analysis Provide clear, easy-to-understand reporting of their process so you can evaluate and verify each step Independent decision-making can save significant time while still leaving final judgment and accountability with you. Source transparency Some AI Technologies provide links to primary sources, inline citations, or a list of reference materials. This allows you to quickly verify claims, trace ideas back to original works, and identify when outputs may over-rely on certain sources. Look for Technologies that can: Provide links or citations to primary sources alongside outputs Show which references were used in generating summaries or analyses Distinguish clearly between retrieved material and generated content Allow you to trace claims back to original context for verification Source transparency strengthens academic rigor by giving you clear visibility into the evidence behind AI outputs and helping you verify sources more efficiently. For more detailed guidance on using AI responsibly in literature reviews, see the Literature review and AI use author FAQ. Workflow compatibility AI Technologies are most effective when they integrate smoothly into your existing research tools and practices. Workflow-compatible tools reduce friction, support collaboration, and let you spend more time focusing on the research itself rather than copying, reformatting, or troubleshooting. Look for Technologies that can: Integrate with your existing writing, reference management, or data analysis software Allow you to export outputs in formats you and your collaborators use Support collaborative workflows, such as sharing conversation logs or annotations Allow you to adjust tone, style, or format to fit academic standards Workflow compatibility ensures AI supports your research without disrupting your established processes. Literature review with AI How can I use AI to support literature review? Wiley supports the purposeful use of AI for literature search and review. AI can make your literature work more efficient and comprehensive through semantic search capabilities that help you discover sources beyond traditional keyword searches. When using AI for literature review, combine AI capabilities with established research practices and your own critical evaluation. Use AI to complement database searches rather than replace them, always asking for primary source information and verifying AI claims by accessing original sources. AI summaries can help introduce you to new topic areas, but it’s recommended that you read full text primary sources for research critical to your work. The image below illustrates an example literature review workflow. As you develop your AI-assisted literature review approach, look for features that provide direct links to original sources and indicate which databases they access. Technologies that connect to paywalled academic content through your institutional access offer more reliable citation verification than those working from limited training data. Be aware that general purpose AI Technologies may generate fabricated or inaccurate references that appear real. Always check whether a citation corresponds to an actual publication. For journal articles, confirm the DOI leads to a valid record in an academic database, your library’s catalog, or the publisher’s website. How can I maintain literature review quality when using AI? To maintain literature review quality when using AI, ensure that your work demonstrates authentic engagement with sources, accurate citations, and genuine scholarly analysis. Be mindful of these editorial standards and expectations: Read relevant primary sources yourself rather than relying solely on AI summaries for papers critical to your work Develop and present original analysis that demonstrates your scholarly engagement, with AI supporting rather than replacing your critical thinking Ensure all citations directly support the specific claims you're making in your manuscript Verify that every citation corresponds to an actual, accessible publication Confirm that cited sources make the arguments you attribute to them Literature reviews serve a vital function in advancing knowledge by providing authentic scholarly synthesis and analysis. When your literature review demonstrates genuine engagement with sources and original critical thinking, it contributes meaningfully to the scholarly conversation and helps guide future research in productive directions. To maintain engagement with established literature while using AI, ask yourself: "Am I still reading papers thoroughly? Do I understand the methodology and findings well enough to discuss them critically? Could I defend my interpretation of this literature in a scholarly discussion?" These questions help ensure you're using AI to enhance rather than replace your scholarly engagement. Drafting and editing manuscripts with AI How can I use AI effectively for manuscript drafting? Wiley supports the purposeful use of AI for manuscript drafting and editing. When submitting to a journal Wiley publishes, you remain fully responsible for the accuracy of your work and accountable for the integrity of any Materials you submit. Current editorial standards expect authors to maintain intellectual control and ownership over their manuscript development. AI can help you explore, refine, or express your research and existing ideas through collaborative and iterative drafting. If you’re unsure if your use of AI for drafting is appropriate, ask yourself the following questions: Am I using my expertise to direct this work? Are the ideas, insights, and analysis my own? Am I consistently reviewing and taking responsibility for the output? Am I selectively implementing AI suggestions myself rather than copying all suggested text directly? Does this read like my authentic voice or follow expected academic writing styles? Does my work communicate my research clearly and concisely? The table below outlines common ways AI can support your writing, along with important considerations and verification recommendations for each use case. Be aware that AI Technologies may retain or train on content you share with them. For more information on privacy and AI Technologies, refer to the Protecting your content when using AI section of this guidance . Use case How AI may be helpful Be aware that... Grammar and language refinement Grammar correction and sentence structure improvement Suggesting more appropriate word choices or formal language Suggestions for rephrasing awkward sentences Translation and support for non-native languages Reducing word count while maintaining meaning AI may suggest incorrect terminology or miss field-specific nuances AI may change your intended meaning during rephrasing Suggestions may not align with your writing style or voice Word count reduction may remove important technical details Recommendation: Verify that AI suggestions preserve your intended meaning and accurately represent your field Document organization and formatting assistance Working through sections or paragraphs to improve readability and flow Assistance with transitions between paragraphs or sections Help organizing sections and improving manuscript structure Formatting support for citations and references Creating first drafts of standard sections like acknowledgments Adapting manuscript formatting and structure to specific journal style requirements AI doesn't understand your specific research logic or discipline conventions Suggested organization may not follow journal requirements Recommendation: Verify that AI- suggested structural changes support your argument Research argument and methodology guidance Asking AI to identify potential gaps in your argument structure Feedback on whether your results follow logically from your methods Help ensuring your discussion connects appropriately to your findings AI cannot assess the research validity of your arguments or data Suggestions may not account for discipline-specific reasoning Recommendation: Use your expertise to evaluate AI recommendations, enlist co-authors or colleagues for human review Messaging and communication strategy Identifying tone inconsistencies across sections Making technical language more accessible to broader audiences Reframing content for different audience impact while preserving meaning Support in converting written or audio notes into formal academic language AI may oversimplify complex concepts when adapting for different audiences Tone changes may alter your intended scholarly impact or authority AI may not understand communication norms specific to your academic field Recommendation: Ensure audience adaptations maintain technical accuracy and appropriate scholarly tone How can I maintain manuscript quality when using AI? Strong manuscripts demonstrate clear purpose, methods, and claims, logical reasoning, and authentic scholarly voice. When using AI for drafting assistance, focus on maintaining these quality indicators regardless of what technology you use to support your manuscript preparation. While editorial standards may vary across journals, these basic expectations for integrity and communication can help you evaluate your AI use: Writing to maintain research integrity AI Technologies can sometimes generate plausible-sounding content that lacks depth, create convincing but nonexistent references, or provide generic terminology that doesn't reflect field-specific expertise. To maintain research integrity when using AI: Provide original analysis and an informed critical perspective rather than superficial discussion Support all claims with appropriate evidence and ensure all referenced literature is real and relevant Interpret statistical results accurately and thoroughly, verifying any AI-generated analysis or interpretation Use precise, field-appropriate research terminology Writing to reach your audience AI may produce text with verbose explanations that are unnecessarily complex or provide suggested text that does not match the overall tone of your document. To maintain clear communication and authentic scholarly voice when using AI: Ensure your text makes logical connections between ideas, methods, and conclusions Provide a strong narrative structure that logically guides readers through your findings Keep explanations focused rather than unnecessarily wordy or repetitive Write in a consistent tone that reflects genuine academic engagement with your subject Data visualization, illustrations, and photographs with AI Wiley supports the purposeful use of AI for creating data visualizations and illustrations. While AI can help you create compelling visual content, important restrictions apply to photograph enhancement to protect research integrity. Wiley also provides the following general Guidelines for the preparation of figures to support authors in the development and submission of all figures associated with their manuscript. Can I use AI to support the creation of data visualizations used in my manuscript submission? Yes. Wiley supports the use of AI for creating data visualizations. Data visualizations include charts, graphs, plots, heat maps, scatter plots, statistical analyses, and any other images that display your numerical research data or measurements.. When using AI for data visualizations, you are required to disclose your AI use and verify the accuracy of your submitted image as outlined in the Disclosure and declaration of AI use Author FAQ. Choosing the right AI Technologies for data visualization When working with complex data visualizations, established data visualization software with AI enabled features or specialized data visualization AI Technologies are more effective than general-purpose large language models. General-purpose language models aren't designed for the precision required in data representation, while specialized data visualization Technologies provide more reliable accuracy. Ensuring accuracy and quality Regardless of which Technologies you use, it is your responsibility to verify that your data representations, statistical interpretations, and visual elements accurately reflect your research. For complex visualizations, consider expert review from a co-author or colleague. All figures should meet publication quality standards including appropriate resolution, readable text size, clear legends, and professional formatting. Can I use AI to support the creation of illustrations used in my manuscript submission? Yes. Wiley supports the use of AI for creating research figures and illustrations. Research figures and illustrations include visual representations of concepts, processes, or systems without displaying your actual research data - such as process flows, workflow diagrams, conceptual frameworks, experimental setup illustrations, and flowcharts. When using AI to generate or edit research figures and illustrations, you are required to disclose your AI use and verify the accuracy of your submitted image as outlined in the Disclosure and declaration of AI use Author FAQ . For generated illustration content, ensure compliance with any licensing requirements as outlined in the Content rights and permissions author FAQ. Choosing the right AI Technologies for illustrations For simple diagrams and conceptual illustrations, general-purpose AI illustration generation Technologies can be effective. However, for complex realistic systems, specialized AI illustration tools designed for scientific content provide more accurate results than general-purpose Technologies. Ensuring accuracy and quality You must verify that AI-generated diagrams accurately represent established knowledge and contain no factual errors.. All figures should meet publication quality standards including appropriate resolution, readable text size, clear legends, and professional formatting. Understanding licensing and permissions AI-generated illustrations can incorporate visual elements from the AI's training data, potentially creating licensing restrictions that don't apply to visualizations of your own research data. AI-generated imagery may be subject to usage restrictions, attribution requirements, or commercial limitations that could affect your publication rights. Always check the terms of use for any AI Technologies used, as even "free" Technologies often have licensing restrictions. Can I use AI to enhance photographs in my manuscript? No. Photographs in research manuscripts serve as evidence and artifacts of your research process. AI enhancement tools can introduce unintended alterations that compromise the reliability and reproducibility of your research evidence. Even when AI improvements appear beneficial, they may alter critical details that other researchers need to evaluate your methods and findings. For legitimate technical adjustments, use established image processing software for minimal, uniform edits such as contrast or color correction and refer to Wiley’s Guidelines for the preparation of figures . Use in research methods AI Technologies may be used to analyze photographs as part of your research methodology. These uses should be disclosed in your methods section and may also be included in your supplemental materials. Why this boundary matters Research integrity investigations have repeatedly identified image manipulation as a significant concern. Maintaining clear standards for photograph processing helps preserve trust in the scholarly record and ensures your research evidence remains reliable for scientific evaluation. Creating marketing content with AI Can I use AI to create promotional content based on my published research? Yes. AI can be a powerful ally in communicating and sharing your research with broader audiences. Many researchers find AI particularly helpful for translating complex findings into accessible language, creating engaging social media content, developing podcast scripts, or drafting blog posts that reach new audiences. How AI can help with research outreach: Audience adaptation: Transform technical or complex language for different audiences – for example: general public, policymakers, or students Format variety: Create content across multiple channels – social media posts, newsletter content, presentation summaries Time efficiency: Generate first drafts quickly, leaving you more time to refine and personalize Creative angles: Explore different ways to frame your findings or identify compelling hooks for your research story Best practices for using AI with research outreach: Check your rights: Review your publishing agreement to confirm whether you can adapt your work into other formats (such as podcasts, blogs, or social media posts). Some agreements place these uses under subsidiary rights and may require publisher approval Protect your content: Do not upload full articles or large portions of copyrighted text unless your agreement explicitly allows it. Use only AI Technologies that have privacy protections, such as “do not train” settings or enterprise-grade security Review for accuracy and tone: AI-generated drafts may oversimplify, misrepresent findings, or adopt a tone that doesn’t fit your audience or your personal communication style. Always review in full and edit for accuracy, clarity, and alignment with your personal communication style and goals Be transparent when needed: If AI played a role in producing outreach materials, acknowledge its use in line with institutional or professional guidelines Methods and AI use in developing this guidance This guidance and all author FAQs were developed through direct engagement with authors and editors. We conducted in-depth interviews with over 40 authors and editors across various disciplines to understand how they are incorporating AI Technologies into their work. These conversations revealed nuanced insights that shaped our guidance and highlighted key questions that authors have about confidently and safely applying AI Technologies in their manuscript development. Our development process: Interviewed research authors and editors across various disciplines Reviewed and analyzed current industry guidelines and publisher policies Drafted initial best practices reflecting author, editor, and industry insights Refined guidance and best practices through iterative additions from Wiley subject matter experts, including team members from Legal, Research Integrity, Publishing, and Technology Edited document for consistency, tone, and clarity Generative AI use in research analysis and content development Interviews were recorded and transcribed utilizing Copilot in Microsoft Teams (February 2025 – June 2025). Interview notes were entered and tagged manually by the researchers in Miro (June – July 2025) and then sorted using the clustering feature. All interviews were either directly conducted by or reviewed by the primary author of this work to ensure familiarity with the interview content prior to introducing generative AI Technologies for analysis and drafting. The drafting authors directed content development through extensive collaboration with Claude 4 Sonnet (July 2025). All content shared with the AI Technology was de-identified to ensure interviewee confidentiality. Notes and direct quotes were shared with Claude 4 Sonnet one theme at a time, along with prior Wiley AI guidance as context for the desired output format and content structure. The drafting authors provided editorial direction for initial section drafts, which were composed by Claude 4 Sonnet based on interview findings. Through iterative rounds of review and revision, all text content was refined and rewritten by the author to ensure accuracy, appropriate tone, and alignment with Wiley's vision for supporting authors in responsible AI use. The drafting authors used Claude 4 Sonnet and GPT-5 (July – August 2025) for comprehensive document review to identify potential gaps in flow, consistency, and completeness. All AI recommendations were evaluated and selectively incorporated based on the research team's editorial judgment and subject matter expertise. All AI-generated content was verified against original interview data to ensure accuracy and authentic representation of researcher insights. Once the initial draft was completed, Wiley experts from various disciplinary fields reviewed and updated the document. This expert review and revision ensures that all proposed guidance meets legal and integrity standards. The final draft was revised by the author using Claude 4 Sonnet and GPT-5 (August 2025) and then polished by a human copyeditor with expertise in research communication. Home Publish Article AI guidelines for researchers Using AI tools in your research A guide for research authors, editors, and reviewers Generative AI tools are rapidly transforming the research landscape, offering new ways to analyze data, refine writing, and accelerate discovery. Whether you're experimenting with these technologies or regularly integrating them into your work, using AI responsibly helps ensure your work remains original, ethical, and aligned with scholarly standards. This guide is designed to support journal authors, editors, and peer reviewers across disciplines in making informed decisions about AI’s role in research and writing. It outlines best practices, clarifies expectations around disclosure and oversight, and addresses common questions about using AI tools while safeguarding intellectual property rights and maintaining the integrity of your research. These guidelines and FAQs apply to journal authors, editors, and peer reviewers. Book authors can find guidance in our AI writing guide for book authors . After reading this document you’ll be able to: Use AI responsibly: Apply best practices and properly disclose AI use in manuscripts and peer review Protect your work: Maintain intellectual property rights and ensure privacy, data protection, and licensing compliance Navigate policies confidently: Understand copyright implications and publisher requirements for AI-generated content Choose the right tools: Evaluate, test, and select appropriate AI technologies while recognizing and mitigating potential biases Apply AI effectively: Leverage AI for literature reviews, manuscript drafting, data visualization, and research promotion while considering environmental sustainability Author guidance on generative AI tools Authors may wish to use artificial intelligence tools or technologies (“AI Technology”) when preparing a manuscript or other material for publication in journals published by Wiley (the “Material”). Wiley welcomes the thoughtful use of AI Technology. When used responsibly, authors can maintain high editorial standards, safeguard intellectual property and other rights, and foster transparency with readers. While Wiley authors remain fully accountable for their Material and any Technologies or sources they use in its creation, Wiley recognizes AI Technology’s growing role in manuscript preparation and provides the following guidance. Review terms and conditions Before choosing the AI Technology that can best support their research and manuscript development, authors should carefully review the terms and conditions, terms of use, or any other conditions or licenses associated with that technology. Authors must confirm that the AI Technology does not claim ownership of their content or impose limitations on its use, as this could interfere with an author’s rights to use specific outputs in their manuscripts, the journal or an academic society’s rights, or Wiley's rights, including Wiley’s ability to publish the Material. Authors should periodically revisit the AI Technology terms to ensure continued compliance. For detailed guidance on evaluating AI tools for privacy and data protection, review the Protecting your content when using AI author FAQs. Human oversight Authors may only use AI Technology as a companion to their writing process, not a replacement. As always, authors must take full responsibility for the accuracy of all content, and verify that all claims, citations, references, and analyses are aligned with their expertise and research. Before including AI-generated content in their Material, authors must carefully review it to ensure the final work reflects their expertise, voice, and originality while adhering to Wiley's ethical and editorial standards. For more information on how to maintain intellectual oversight while using AI Technology, review the Maintaining Intellectual Leadership with AI Technology author FAQs. Disclosure Authors should maintain documentation of all AI Technology used, including its purpose, whether it impacted key arguments or conclusions, and how they personally reviewed and verified AI-generated content. Authors must disclose the use of AI Technologies when submitting their Material to a Wiley-published journal. If not provided, the journal may request this documentation. Transparency is essential to Wiley's commitment to integrity in publishing . For information on how to declare your use of AI Technologies, review the Disclosure and declaration of AI use author FAQs. Rights protection Authors must not use any AI Technology that restricts their own, Wiley’s, or any other party’s use of the Material. This includes ensuring that the AI Technology used and the provider of that AI Technology does not gain any rights over the author’s underlying content, including the right to ingest or “train” their AI Technology on the content, besides the limited right to access and use the Material to perform the service. By reviewing an AI Technology’s terms and conditions for clauses such as “ownership”, “data reuse”, or “opt out” among others, authors can prevent unintended rights transfers. For further guidance on evaluating AI tools for rights protection, review the Content Rights and Permissions author FAQs. Responsible and ethical use Authors must use AI Technology in a manner that aligns with privacy, confidentiality, and compliance obligations. This includes respecting data protection laws, avoiding the use of AI to replicate unique styles or voices of others, and fact-checking AI-generated content for accuracy and neutrality. Authors should be aware of potential biases in AI outputs and take steps to mitigate stereotypes or misinformation. When inputting sensitive or unpublished content, authors should use Technologies with appropriate privacy controls to protect confidentiality. For more information on responsible and ethical use of AI Technology, review the Protecting your content when using AI author FAQs and Bias and Equity Considerations author FAQs. Adherence to agreements Authors must adhere to the terms and conditions of their agreement with Wiley. Authors remain responsible for upholding their contractual representations, such as ensuring their Material is original, not previously published, and that they have the right to grant the necessary permissions to Wiley as set forth in their agreement. Wiley values authors' unique creativity and expertise and views AI Technologies as tools that enhance rather than replace creativity. Wiley remains committed to providing clear guidance that fosters trust with readers, protects authors' and Wiley’s rights, and ensures high-quality content. These guidelines augment Wiley’s Best practice guidelines on research integrity and publishing ethics and will evolve as technology and author needs advance. This guidance is not intended as legal advice. Authors should consult their representatives with specific questions. Common author questions and best practices These author FAQs (frequently asked questions) and best practices were developed through direct engagement with Wiley journal authors and editors. Our conversations with authors revealed nuanced insights into how they are using generative AI Technologies and highlighted key questions about safely applying these Technologies in their work. For more information on our process and the use of AI Technologies in developing these FAQs and best practices, see our FAQ methods and AI declaration section. Protecting your content while using AI Your research and writing represent significant intellectual contributions to your field. When you use AI Technologies to support manuscript drafting, you may introduce risks such as unintended exposure of your unpublished findings, accidental sharing of confidential data, diminished intellectual property rights, or other ethical concerns. Understanding how to evaluate Technologies — and which practices can reduce these risks—will help you protect both your work and your responsibilities as a researcher. How should I evaluate AI Technologies for data and intellectual property risks? AI Technologies differ significantly in how they handle user data. User data includes any content you type into the tool, any datasets, figures, or manuscript sections you upload, as well as any output generated by the tool. Some AI Technologies collect, store, or train on this content by default, while others offer stronger protections. Your content protection needs may vary depending on the project type, institutional or funder requirements, publishing or other intellectual property agreements, or data use and confidentiality agreements. The table below outlines what to look for when evaluating AI Technologies to protect your research and data. Category Look for AI Technologies that: Be wary of AI Technologies that: Content ownership and rights Explicitly state they do not claim ownership or rights over your content Place no restrictions on your ability to use your content or the outputs generated Claim broad rights over your content, using terms like “perpetual” or “transferable” Use language such as “royalty-free” or “for any purpose” that reduce your control over how your content and outputs are used Place restrictions on your ability to use your own content or the outputs generated Data training and model use Allow you to opt out of data retention and training, or default to no retention Clearly limit how your content can be used (e.g., only to deliver the service you request, not for training or redistribution) Automatically include your content in model training without your explicit consent Classify outputs as “open,” “free to use” (which can open your work to unrestricted use), or default them under licenses like Creative Commons Data storage and sharing Provide clear options for deleting your data Retain your data indefinitely or share it with third parties without clear limits Legal terms about rights and usage may be found in the AI Technologies' terms of service, while data practices are typically in privacy policies. Some privacy controls (like opting out of training) may be available in the settings menu. Remember, you remain the rights holder to your research and writing—unless you give those rights away. Protect your intellectual contributions by choosing Technologies and practices that keep them under your control. What if I have access to an enterprise version of an AI Technology? Institutions may offer enterprise versions of common AI Technologies or private installations of AI models that run within their own environment. These versions should offer stronger protection than consumer Technologies, such as: Explicit terms ensuring your content is not used for training Clearer data retention and deletion policies Content ownership guarantees Institutional compliance with funder, ethics, and data protection rules Enhanced administrative controls (e.g., disabling sharing outside the institution, single sign-on security) It’s likely that these are generally the safest option for working with unpublished or sensitive research material, though you’ll need to review the terms of use and follow your institution’s guidelines for AI use. What research privacy practices should I follow when using AI? The privacy and data protection practices you already follow in your research still apply when using AI Technologies. The main difference is that these tools may handle your data in ways you can’t fully see or control. Extend your existing safeguards by keeping in mind: If you are reviewing a manuscript as a peer reviewer or journal editor: Do not upload any portion of submitted manuscripts, as these are confidential to the peer review process If your research involves human subjects: Consult your Institutional Review Board (IRB) to determine if using AI Technologies with participant data requires additional approval. You may also need to review whether your original participant consent forms cover sharing data with third-party AI services. Note that photographs with identifiable individuals are especially sensitive, and extreme care should be taken to protect personally identifiable participant data from third-party AI services If your work involves data from individuals or private entities: Remove personally identifiable information before using non-enterprise AI tools. If using enterprise AI tools with appropriate privacy controls, it may be possible to work with identifiable data when it aligns with institutional data governance policies, IRB approval (where applicable), and any consent or compliance requirements. For example, some enterprise tools could be used to assist with de-identification by replacing identifiable information with anonymized identifiers, or to support qualitative analysis of interview transcripts. If you work with embargoed data: Follow any explicit restrictions on sharing embargoed or proprietary content When working with your own drafts or datasets : Use your professional judgement when determining what to share with AI. Consider working with smaller text segments and substituting key details to maintain control over confidentiality while using AI to support your work Maintaining intellectual leadership with AI Technology Your expertise is what enables you to derive value from AI technology. Having foundational skills in the area where you want AI assistance helps you better direct the AI to produce useful output and to more efficiently evaluate AI output for quality. Consider your readiness across these key areas: Directing AI effectively: Do you understand the underlying principles for the task at hand well enough that you can strategically direct the AI to support your work? Evaluating AI outputs: Can you identify when AI outputs are inaccurate for your field or don’t meet professional standards? Do you have time to validate output for areas where you aren’t currently an expert? AI as a research assistant Think of AI as a research assistant working under your direction, helping you accomplish your goals more efficiently. You’re responsible for how you use AI to support your work, and accountable for the quality, accuracy, and integrity of your research and manuscript. When working with AI, your role is to: Define your goals and expectations for your AI collaboration Provide directions to the AI based on your specific needs Evaluate outputs using your domain knowledge Seek input from AI, then implement changes in the work yourself Make final decisions about what to include in your work Disclose your use of AI transparently in your manuscript so readers can understand your process AI for productivity and efficiency AI can be an effective productivity tool for tasks like reference formatting, language polishing, data organization, and standard code generation. When using AI for efficiency, ask yourself: Will this save me time and effort? Does the output meet my standards for quality? Am I confident I can verify and take responsibility for what the AI produces? Does this application present potential integrity or ethical concerns? AI and critical thinking Consider your intent before using AI. When using AI for tasks related to research or manuscripts, understanding how it can enhance or hinder your critical thinking helps you make strategic choices. Building expertise requires working through challenges, making mistakes, and problem-solving independently. These are things that AI cannot replace. AI can enhance your critical thinking when used to: Support your initial learning journey for new topics or domains Expand your thinking by generating multiple ideas or perspectives to evaluate Identify and explore patterns across different studies or datasets Challenge your assumptions and identify gaps or bias in thinking If you have concerns about becoming over reliant on AI, avoid: Using AI to make decisions rather than to inform your judgment Relying solely on AI summaries instead of engaging with key primary sources Incorporating AI suggestions without understanding their rationale Using AI to bypass skill-building opportunities Choosing when NOT to use AI AI Technologies may not be right for every task or research project. Decide strategically where these tools genuinely support your research goals, and where established methods—the research approaches, tools, and techniques you currently use—may be more effective, accurate, or better aligned with professional standards. Consider relying on established methods when: Building expertise and understanding You want to understand underlying concepts or develop core skills, and need to work through challenges independently to build expertise Efficiency, quality, or accuracy favor established methods You already have a trusted tool that reliably and effectively meets your needs You are working on a task that requires high precision and accuracy You’re working in an unfamiliar domain where verifying the AI outputs would take more time and effort than using established methods Constraints or values make AI a poor fit The task involves sensitive, unpublished, or confidential data Institutional or funding requirements limit or restrict AI use The verification effort required would be substantial You want to maintain full control over your research process Established methods align better with your research values and goals To make informed choices about when and how to use AI, it helps to understand the basics of how these tools work and what to look for when evaluating them. For more information on types of AI Technologies and their capabilities, see the Getting started with AI Technologies author FAQ. Disclosure and declaration of AI use Where should I disclose AI use in my manuscript? AI disclosure should be integrated into your manuscript using the same logic you apply to other tools and assistance. For journals published by Wiley, AI disclosure should be placed in the following sections of your manuscript: In your Acknowledgments when AI assists with manuscript drafting, editing, translation, or formatting. In your Methods when AI is used to assist with your research methodology, data collection or analysis, or literature review processes. In your figure captions when AI generates or edits any visual content that appears in your manuscript. This ensures that disclosure appears where readers, editors, and reviewers naturally expect to find information about tools and processes used in your work. How should I disclose the use of AI for drafting and editing assistance? Wiley requires disclosure of AI when used to generate substantial text or restructure arguments. Disclosure ensures transparency in manuscript evaluation and accountability by the authors for the content, a generally held authorship requirement. Simple grammar correction, formatting, and word choice suggestions typically do not require disclosure. The table below illustrates how these criteria apply to common AI use scenarios for manuscript development. These examples demonstrate the distinction between AI assistance that creates or transforms content (requiring disclosure) versus AI that polishes existing work (typically no disclosure needed). AI use category Requires disclosure Does not require disclosure Drafting and editing Text generation: AI generates or substantially rephrases text for the manuscript; creates first drafts of standard sections (e.g., acknowledgments); converts notes or audio into formal academic language Translation: AI translates manuscript sections (e.g., abstract, methods) into another language Cohesion and organization: AI restructures arguments or document flow; improves readability and flow of paragraphs; assists with transitions; reorganizes sections of the manuscript Argument and logic: AI identifies gaps in argument structure; provides feedback on whether results follow from methods; ensures discussion connects to findings Tone and audience: AI identifies tone inconsistencies across sections; reframes content for different audiences while preserving meaning Language polishing: AI corrects grammar and spelling; suggests word choices; rephrases awkward sentences Conciseness: AI reduces word count while maintaining meaning Formatting and compliance: AI formats citations and references; adapts manuscript style to journal requirements; checks formatting and readability When disclosing AI use in your acknowledgements, we recommend sharing: AI Technology name and version Date/year of use The AI Technology's role in your work (language translation, substantial editing or text generation, organization of document or argument flow, etc.) Specific sections where the AI was used in the document The author’s role in directing or reviewing the work of the AI Technology Example disclosure statements The following examples illustrate how you might disclose AI use in your acknowledgements. Adapt the language as needed to reflect the Technologies used in your manuscript development and their roles in your work. "The authors acknowledge the use of Microsoft Copilot (accessed August 2024) to generate a first draft of the Acknowledgments section and translate the Methods section from Spanish to English. Gemini (accessed July 2025) was used to reorganize the Discussion section for clarity and flow. All AI-assisted text was reviewed and revised by the authors to ensure accuracy and clarity of meaning.” “The authors acknowledge the use of Claude 3.5 Sonnet (accessed July 2025) to support creation of the abstract and to suggest reorganization of Discussion section for clarity and flow. All AI-generated suggestions were reviewed, revised, and approved by the authors, who take full responsibility for the accuracy and integrity of the work.” How should I disclose the use of AI in my research methodology? Wiley requires disclosure when AI is used in your research methodology, including study design and data collection, literature review, code development, and data analysis. Disclosure of AI methods builds upon the established research practice of documenting your process so others can understand, evaluate, and build upon your work. The details of your disclosure should match the importance of the AI Technology’s role in your work. The following table illustrates common scenarios where AI use should be disclosed. These examples are illustrative, not exhaustive. When in doubt, ask yourself: “would a reviewer need to know about this AI use to properly evaluate my research or manuscript?” AI use category Requires disclosure Does not require disclosure Study design and data collection Study design: AI generates survey questions based on research objectives Image processing: AI processes medical images or microscopy data for analysis Protocol development: AI develops or expands experimental protocols by analyzing existing methods Infrastructure support: AI creates databases, patient registries, or study recruitment platforms Organization: AI organizes experimental protocols in notebooks or databases Troubleshooting: AI helps troubleshoot software errors Learning support: AI explains research methodology concepts for learning purposes Formatting: AI helps format research materials or templates Literature review Agentic search: AI independently generates or executes search strategies (designing full search strings, running database queries, or pre-filtering results) for a literature review or systematic review Screening and selection: AI applies inclusion/exclusion criteria to filter or select literature for systematic reviews or meta-analyses Synthesis (used in manuscript): AI clusters studies, identifies themes, or generates comparative insights across multiple papers when included in the manuscript Citation extraction: AI pulls references or metadata from papers or databases Search support: AI assists with keyword or phrase-based searching to suggest relevant resources; the author reviews and decides on final inclusion Reference management: AI organizes citations and references Reference formatting: AI assists with citation style formatting Literature summarization: AI generates summaries of papers to support author understanding of dense or lengthy content Code development Programming: AI writes analysis scripts or algorithms Software generation: AI develops custom research software or applications Data pipeline design: AI builds automated workflows to clean, process, and integrate datasets Modeling: AI develops or adapts computational or statistical models for research analysis Debugging: AI checks syntax errors or suggests fixes Code style: AI formats code structure/style Documentation: AI generates comments or documentation Suggestions: AI recommends standard library functions or common approaches Data and analysis Qualitative analysis: AI codes interview or textual data; identifies patterns or themes in large datasets Quantitative/statistical analysis: AI performs statistical analyses, flags outliers that alter conclusions, interprets results of tests Exploratory or advanced analyses: AI generates interpretations of results, highlights relationships or trends in data, or synthesizes findings across datasets Routine transformations: AI converts units, standardizes formats, or executes simple data transformations Data cleaning: AI assists with data entry or cleaning (e.g., removing duplicates, fixing typos) Once you’ve identified AI use that requires disclosure, provide enough detail so reviewers and readers can evaluate your process and, where appropriate, reproduce your work. When disclosing AI use in your methods, we require that you share: AI Technology name and version Date/year of use The AI Technology’s role in your work (image processing, code development, statistical analysis, etc.) The author’s role in directing or reviewing the work of the AI Technology For sensitive, proprietary, or human subject data describe how privacy and compliance requirements were maintained Example disclosure statements The following examples illustrate how you might disclose AI use in your methods. Adapt the language as needed to reflect the Technologies used in your research and their roles in your work. "Literature screening was assisted by GPT-4 (accessed March 2024) to categorize 500+ abstracts by predefined relevance criteria. AI-assisted outputs were reviewed and validated by the authors, who confirmed the final inclusion and exclusion decisions.” “Analysis scripts for regression models were generated using Claude 3.5 Sonnet (accessed June 2025). All AI-generated code was reviewed, tested, and revised by the authors, who confirmed accuracy and reproducibility of results.” “AI-assisted analysis was conducted using LLaMA 3 (accessed May 2025) to extract themes from qualitative interview data. All AI-generated outputs were reviewed and validated by the authors, who confirmed the accuracy of the interpretations. Prompts and system settings are included in Supplementary File S2 for reproducibility. All data were de-identified in compliance with institutional privacy standards prior to analysis.” If AI contributed substantially to your analysis or methodology, you may be asked to provide supplemental documentation to support reproducibility and transparency, such as: Prompts or parameters used AI settings or configurations Code repository Data protection approach Compliance with institutional requirements How should I disclose the use of AI Technologies in manuscript visuals? Wiley requires disclosure when AI is used for visual creation or editing. The following table illustrates common scenarios where AI use should be disclosed. These examples are illustrative, not exhaustive. AI use category Requires disclosure Does not require disclosure Visuals Data visualizations: AI generates charts, graphs, plots, heat maps, scatter plots, or statistical figures using a researcher’s underlying data Research illustrations: AI generates or edits workflow diagrams, conceptual frameworks, visualization of experimental setups, or flowcharts Creation of composite images: AI is used to create composite images collected during the course of your research; this should be clearly explained in the image caption or legend. Original images may be requested upon submission Data visualization exploration: AI supports exploration of data visualization or diagram ideas that do not end up in the manuscript Research illustration exploration: AI supports exploration of illustrations that do not end up in the manuscript or are fully recreated in established software AI-edited photographs are not permitted in Wiley journals. However, AI may be used to analyze photographs you have collected during the course of your research as part of your research methodology. These uses must be disclosed in the methods section including the software used for transparency and reproducibility. Factual and evidential images are not permitted. These images support specific scientific, clinical, or technical claims (e.g., research results, experiential data, diagnostic images). AI Technologies must not be used to generate, modify, or enhance these images, as they require verifiable accuracy. When disclosing AI we require that you share: AI Technology name and version Date/year of use The AI Technology's role in your work The author’s role in directing, reviewing, or editing the AI-generated output Example disclosure statements The following examples illustrate how you might disclose AI use in your image captions. Adapt the language as needed to reflect the AI Technologies used. “Figure 1. Conceptual diagram of treatment pathway. Diagram created using DALL·E 3 (accessed March 2024) to generate an initial draft, with subsequent modifications by the authors in Canva. All content was reviewed for accuracy by the clinical team.” “Figure 2. Cellular signaling pathway. Draft diagram created in BioRender with AI-assisted layout suggestions (accessed April 2025). Final image refined and annotated by the authors. All pathway details were cross-checked against experimental data.” These disclosures do not replace your obligation to comply with the AI Technology’s terms and conditions or your obligation to ensure the accuracy of the visual or image. To learn more about this, refer to the Content rights and permissions author FAQ. For guidance on approved and unapproved visual content in Wiley journals, see the Data visualization, illustrations, and photographs with AI author FAQ . If you have doubts about specific scenarios or uses not covered here, err on the side of disclosure. How should co-authors coordinate disclosure of AI use? Multi-authored papers involve shared responsibility for content and methodology, making transparent communication about AI use important for each co-author. We recommend discussing AI use as a team early in the process, especially where author contributions are clearly delineated in the manuscript. Agreeing on what tools were used, by whom, and how disclosure will be worded helps ensure your manuscript is both accurate and transparent. What does Wiley do with AI disclosure information? Supporting the editorial process Your disclosure helps editors and reviewers properly evaluate AI-assisted research methodology and ensures reviewers have the context they need to assess reproducibility. This information also helps us identify what new competencies our editorial teams and reviewers need to properly assess AI-assisted research, enabling us to provide targeted training on evaluating AI methodology, verification approaches, and research integrity considerations. Advancing research practices We analyze anonymized disclosure data to identify effective AI use strategies and develop targeted guidance and resources for different research disciplines. This analysis helps us share insights about successful AI integration in research workflows and contributes to broader conversations about AI standards in scholarly publishing. Building community knowledge Your disclosure helps other researchers learn from innovative AI applications and informs the development of subject-specific AI guidance. This collective knowledge supports the evolution of responsible AI use in research and contributes to reproducibility and research integrity standards across the research community. Your disclosure isn't just about compliance—it's about transparency and contributing to the collective advancement of research practices. The insights we gain help us better identify where training is needed for editorial teams and reviewers, and contributes to the development of AI standards in scholarly publishing. Content rights and permissions As an author publishing in a scholarly journal, it’s important to understand how copyright protects your work and enables publication. Copyright gives the holder of that right (such as an author, or publisher, or employer) the exclusive rights to control and grant permission on how their creative works, such as published articles, are used, reproduced, and distributed. Creators automatically retain copyright in their original work from the moment of creation. However, most jurisdictions set forth that copyright requires human authorship and does not allow AI to be considered an author. Where AI Technologies are used, there must be sufficient human contribution of creative, expressive elements in the final work in order to achieve copyright protection. Authors (or their institutions or employers) must own or have obtained copyright to their work in order to publish with Wiley. This copyright ownership is essential because the copyright holder must then transfer or license these rights to the journal owner which could be Wiley or an academic society or publishing partner through a Copyright Transfer, Exclusive License, or a Creative Commons agreement upon manuscript acceptance. In cases where an employer owns the copyright to an employee's work, the employer can grant Wiley the necessary rights for publication. All authors (or their employer(s) as applicable) must execute one of these agreements upon manuscript acceptance granting Wiley the rights needed to publish, distribute, and make the work available to the scholarly community. Wiley's agreements include AI-specific terms that hold authors responsible for the accuracy of any AI-assisted content and give journal editors final authority over whether AI use is appropriate. How can I avoid copyright issues when using AI in my manuscript? Copyright infringement risks : As an author, you are responsible for ensuring that all content in your article can be legally published and is not subject to any restrictions on use by Wiley, pursuant to the author agreement. AI Technologies may inadvertently reproduce copyrighted material owned by third parties when generating text or images because they are often trained on copyrighted content. This creates a higher risk of copyright infringement and plagiarism than when AI is used to edit your existing work. Potential copyright infringement can be difficult to detect because the original source material may not be easily identifiable in AI outputs. Using copyrighted material without permission could result in mandatory revisions leading to publication delays, manuscript rejection, or retraction or even to legal claims for infringement.
